title: On Fairness of Unified Multimodal Large Language Model for Image Generation
authors: Ming LiuHao ChenJindong WangLiwen WangBhiksha Raj RamakrishnanWensheng Zhang
abstract: Unified multimodal large language models (U-MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in visual understanding and generation in an end-to-end pipeline. Compared with generation-only models (e.g., Stable Diffusion), U-MLLMs may raise new questions about bias in their outputs, which can be affected by their unified capabilities. This gap is particularly concerning given the under-explored risk of propagating harmful stereotypes. In this paper, we benchmark the latest U-MLLMs and find that most of them exhibit significant demographic biases, such as gender and race bias. To better understand and mitigate this issue, we propose a locate-then-fix strategy, where we audit show how the individual model component is affected by bias. Our analysis shows that bias originates primarily from the language model. More interestingly, we observe a “partial alignment” phenomenon in U-MLLMs, where understanding bias appears minimal, but generation bias remains substantial. Thus, we propose a novel balanced preference loss to balance the demographic distribution with synthetic data. Experiments demonstrate that our approach reduces demographic bias while preserving semantic fidelity. We hope our finding underscores the need for more holistic interpretation and debiasing strategies of U-MLLMs in the future.

paragraphs: Multimodal Large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in visual understanding (Li et al., 2024c; Wang et al., 2024a). Recent research (Wu et al., 2024b, a) has focused on extending MLLMs’ capabilities to image generation settings, enabling them to produce textual content and visual content. These unified MLLMs (U-MLLMs), e.g., VILA-U (Wu et al., 2024b), present both visual understanding and generation capability, where they can not only understand the semantics in images but also generate images with high-quality conditioning on user prompts in natural language. However, these U-MLLMs with unified capabilities may inadvertently reproduce or amplify biases at deployment, including gender and racial stereotypes embedded in their large-scale training data (Elazar et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024).

A common structure in U-MLLMs is an image tokenizer that transforms images into discrete tokens via an encoder-decoder framework (Esser et al., 2021). Specifically, the vision encoder compresses the input image into latent embeddings and then quantizes them into discrete tokens. Afterward, a decoder reconstructs the image from these tokens. This discrete tokenization bridges textual and visual modalities by analogizing image tokens to words in a sentence, enabling a single autoregressive objective that unifies text and image generation. While this design has proven effective for quality and scalability, it also opens additional channels for bias from the tokenizer.

Existing work on debiasing in image generation has highlighted the social risks posed by skewed output distributions, and various methods have been proposed to reduce bias in image generation (Chuang et al., 2023; Bansal et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2024c; Gandikota et al., 2024). Nevertheless, many such methods are designed specifically for diffusion models, which leverage different principles for image generation (Shen et al., 2024; Gandikota et al., 2024). As U-MLLMs with autoregressive image generation capabilities become more and more prevalent, it is imperative to evaluate their bias in image generation and develop new methods to reduce bias in these token-based generation models. Moreover, it remains an open question whether the generation biases emerge more from the vision encoder, which generates image tokens, or from the language modeling component, which generates image tokens according to the given text prompt.

This paper investigates and mitigates demographic bias in U-MLLMs for text-to-image generation. Specifically, we made the first step to study gender and race bias for U-MLLMs. We benchmark the latest U-MLLM on gender and race bias using datasets and metrics introduced by a recent study of image generation fairness (Shen et al., 2024). These models comprehensively include VILA-U (Wu et al., 2024b), Show-o (Xie et al., 2024), Janus (Wu et al., 2024a), Janus-Pro (Chen et al., 2025), TokenFlow (Qu et al., 2024), Emu3 (Wang et al., 2024b). Our results show that the latest UMLLMs exhibit notable gender and race biases in image generation (see an example in Figure 1). Next, we conduct a detailed audit of the vision encoder/decoder and the language model component to localize these biases’ source(s), where we find that the biases are mainly from the language model. Finally, we synthesize high-quality training data with a balanced demographic distribution and propose a novel balance preference loss to mitigate generation bias in U-MLLMs, inspired by recent research on direct preference optimization (Rafailov et al., 2024; Hong et al., 2024).

Through extensive experiments with various U-MLLMs, we demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces biases (e.g., over-generation of certain genders or races) without sacrificing the quality of image generation output. For example, for the VILA-U model, our method reduces its gender bias by 71.91% and increases the inception score by 12.2%. In summary, our key contributions are:

• Benchmarking Bias: We benchmark the latest U-MLLMs on race and gender bias and find that they often display it with different degrees for image generation. Notably, Janus-Pro, the latest U-MLLM, witnessed the worst gender bias with a value of 0.90, compared with the stable diffusion model with a bias value of 0.67.

• Localizing Bias: We inspect different components in the VILA-U model (vision encoder, language model) by using methods such as linear probing in image embedding space to pinpoint potential sources of bias and find that the bias is mostly from the language model component.

• Mitigating Bias: We used the diffusion model to synthesize training data with a balanced demographic distribution. We also introduce a balanced preference loss inspired by direct preference optimization, with the objective of balancing the likelihood of visual generation towards the different demographic groups. We empirically show that our approach yields substantial improvement in demographic fairness while preserving the quality of image generation, thus providing a practical framework for developing unified MLLMs with more fairness.

subsections.title: Structure of U-MLLMs
paragraphs: We consider an autoregressive U-MLLM that, given a textual prompt xx, first converts it into a sequence of text tokens {x1,…,xTx}\{x_{1},\dots,x_{T_{x}}\} and then generates a sequence of image tokens {z1,…,zTz}\{z_{1},\dots,z_{T_{z}}\}, which an image decoder reconstructs into a final image yy (see Figure 5 for the pipeline). Let 𝜽={𝜽v,𝜽l}\boldsymbol{\theta}=\{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{v}},\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{l}}\} denote the model parameters, where: (1) 𝜽v\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{v}} is the image tokenizer (encoder-decoder) responsible for converting input images into discrete tokens and decoding tokens back into images. (2)𝜽l\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{l}} is the language model (LM) that processes and generates token sequences (both text and image tokens) in a unified autoregressive way.

As shown in Figure 6, the image encoder E𝜽vE_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{v}}} maps an image yy into latent embeddings e, then quantizes the embeddings into a discrete token sequence {z1,…,zTz}\{z_{1},\dots,z_{T_{z}}\}. Conversely, the image decoder D𝜽vD_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{v}}} inverts this process:
Meanwhile, the LM L​M𝜽lLM_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\mathrm{l}}} treats both text tokens and image tokens uniformly under a single next-token probability:

This design allows the U-MLLM to perform visual understanding by mapping an image to a semantic space (via Eq. 1) and then interpreting those discrete tokens as inputs to the LM, and to perform image generation by autoregressively sampling {zt}\{z_{t}\} from Eq. 3 and reconstructing an image from the resulting token sequence via Eq. 2.

subsections.title: Demographic Bias.
paragraphs: When conditioning on neutral generation prompt (no explicit gender or race specified), the model could exhibit demographic bias by disproportionately generating output with a distribution skewing towards certain demographic groups (e.g., “male” or “female,” “Asian” or “Black”). Figure 1 shows such an example where a prompt “Please generate images of construction workers” yield mostly images samples of one demographic group.

Formally, let d∈𝒟d\in\mathcal{D} where D={d1,…,dK}D=\{d_{1},\ldots,d_{K}\} is a set of demographic labels (e.g., {male, female} or {Asian, Black, White, Indian}). Given a neutral prompt xx, such as “a portrait of a construction worker”, an unbiased model would generate a list of images {y1,y2,…}\{y_{1},y_{2},\ldots\} with these demographic labels in a balanced distribution, for example, 50-50 for gender, or a uniform distribution for race. By contrast, we find that the latest U-MLLMs generate

where di,djd_{i},d_{j} corresponds to “male”,“female” respectively(in this case) and 𝒞\mathcal{C} is a pre-trained image classifier from prior study (Shen et al., 2024) that labels each image yy with a predicted attribute d^i\hat{d}_{i}. This indicated a strong bias in gender preferences. Our goal is to mitigate this bias while preserving overall image fidelity.

subsections.title: Direct Preference Optimization
paragraphs: As an alternative to computational expensive RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022) methods that are required to train a reward model separately, DPO re-parameterizes reward model rr via a ratio between the policy πθ\pi_{\theta} and a reference policy πref\pi_{\text{ref}} and eliminates the cost of training a reward function r​(x,y)r(x,y) explicitly (Rafailov et al., 2024). Concretely, for a given pair (x,y)(x,y),

where Z​(x)Z(x) is a partition function and β\beta is a scaling factor(Rafailov et al., 2024). By plugging rr into a Bradley-Terry model(Bradley & Terry, 1952), DPO formulates the preference probability for a winning response ywy_{w} over a losing response yly_{l} as
DPO aims to optimize the policy directly by minimizing Equation 5 and increasing the difference in models’ preferences between ywy_{w} and yly_{l} without a separate reward model.

To further reduce the overhead of the reference model, recent work explores reference-free preference optimization (Hong et al., 2024; Meng et al., 2024). One representative method, ORPO (Hong et al., 2024), defines the odds of generating a response yy given prompt xx as
and further quantifies the odds ratio between two responses ywy_{w} and yly_{l} via
and finally, define a preference loss term as

By encouraging a large odds ratio ORθ​(yw,yl)\text{OR}_{\theta}(y_{w},y_{l}), the model is pushed to prefer the response ywy_{w} over yly_{l} directly, without relying on a separate “reference” model.

As shown in Figure 7, to identify the biases that might emerge from where, we analyze intermediate outputs - that is, the image tokens (produced by the LM) and the image embeddings (produced by the vision encoder) during the decoding step. We consider two main hypotheses regarding the origin of demographic bias as follows.

subsections.title: 3.1 Hypothesis I: The Bias is from Vision Encoder
paragraphs: The vision encoder transforms input images into a high-dimensional embedding space, as illustrated in Figure 6. This encoder itself may be a source of bias within U-MLLMs. To test this hypothesis, we focus on auditing the vision encoder, which is trained symmetrically alongside the decoder. By detecting the embedding output from the encoder, we aim to identify any inherent biases that could influence the overall fairness of the model.

Figure 7 illustrated the overall pipeline to audit vision encoder: first, we sample a balanced set of images denoted as yi{y_{i}} from FairFace(Kärkkäinen & Joo, 2019) covering different demographic attributes (e.g., male, female). By feeding these images into the vision encoder, we obtained a set of image embeddings denoted as 𝐄={𝐞i}\mathbf{E}=\{\mathbf{e}_{i}\}. Each embedding 𝐞𝐢\mathbf{e_{i}} is labeled with the ground truth attribute di∈{male,female}d_{i}\in\{\text{male},\text{female}\} according to its corresponding image input yiy_{i}. We then split 𝐄\mathbf{E} into training and testing subsets and train a linear classifier ℓ​(⋅)\ell(\cdot) to predict demographic labels from embeddings eie_{i}. This gives us pairs (yi,d^i,𝐞i)\bigl{(}y_{i},\hat{d}_{i},\mathbf{e}_{i}\bigr{)}—i.e. the image, the predicted demographic label, and the corresponding image embedding. The precision, F1 score, recall and precision in the test set are all high, indicating that the encoder’s latent space preserves explicit demographic distinctions:

Since the decoder is trained to invert the encoder, it should faithfully preserve these attributes. We also find that when we prompt the model with an attribute (e.g., “Asian professor”), the final generated images are overwhelmingly associated with that attribute. This also implies that the decoder consistently reflects whatever demographic semantics are either present in the tokens or passed by the encoder. Therefore, the chance that bias originated from the vision encoder is small.

subsections.title: Linear Probing on Image Embeddings
paragraphs: Figure 7 illustrated the overall pipeline to audit vision encoder: first, we sample a balanced set of images denoted as yi{y_{i}} from FairFace(Kärkkäinen & Joo, 2019) covering different demographic attributes (e.g., male, female). By feeding these images into the vision encoder, we obtained a set of image embeddings denoted as 𝐄={𝐞i}\mathbf{E}=\{\mathbf{e}_{i}\}. Each embedding 𝐞𝐢\mathbf{e_{i}} is labeled with the ground truth attribute di∈{male,female}d_{i}\in\{\text{male},\text{female}\} according to its corresponding image input yiy_{i}. We then split 𝐄\mathbf{E} into training and testing subsets and train a linear classifier ℓ​(⋅)\ell(\cdot) to predict demographic labels from embeddings eie_{i}. This gives us pairs (yi,d^i,𝐞i)\bigl{(}y_{i},\hat{d}_{i},\mathbf{e}_{i}\bigr{)}—i.e. the image, the predicted demographic label, and the corresponding image embedding. The precision, F1 score, recall and precision in the test set are all high, indicating that the encoder’s latent space preserves explicit demographic distinctions:

Since the decoder is trained to invert the encoder, it should faithfully preserve these attributes. We also find that when we prompt the model with an attribute (e.g., “Asian professor”), the final generated images are overwhelmingly associated with that attribute. This also implies that the decoder consistently reflects whatever demographic semantics are either present in the tokens or passed by the encoder. Therefore, the chance that bias originated from the vision encoder is small.

subsections.title: 3.2 Hypothesis II: The bias is from the language model
paragraphs: Let xneutralx_{\text{neutral}} be a neutral prompt (e.g., “a photo of a professor”), and let xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}) denote an augmented version of the same prompt specifying a particular demographic did_{i} (e.g., “a photo of a female professor”). Assume that for each xx, we sample a list of images {y1,…,yM}\{y_{1},\ldots,y_{M}\} and each image yiy_{i} is associated with a sequence of image tokens generated from LM as 𝐳i=(zi,1,zi,2,…)\mathbf{z}_{i}=(z_{i,1},z_{i,2},\ldots).

We record the intermediate image tokens for (1) The neutral prompt xneutralx_{\text{neutral}} and (2) The demographic-augmented prompts xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}) as described above. We then use a pre-trained image classifier 𝒞\mathcal{C} (Shen et al., 2024) to label each final image yiy_{i} with a predicted attribute d^i=𝒞​(yi)\widehat{d}_{i}\;=\;\mathcal{C}(y_{i}). This yields pairs (xi,yi,d^i,𝐳i)\bigl{(}x_{i},y_{i},\hat{d}_{i},\mathbf{z}_{i}\bigr{)}—i.e. the prompt, the resulting image, the predicted demographic label, and the corresponding image token sequence.
Let pθ​(z∣x)p_{\theta}(z\mid x) denote the probability distribution over the image tokens zz given a prompt xx. In practice, we approximate it by a finite sample:

where δ​(⋅)\delta(\cdot) is a Kronecker delta that counts the sampled sequences {𝐳i}\{\mathbf{z}_{i}\}. This empirical approximation estimates the token distribution pθ​(z∣x)p_{\theta}(z\mid x) by averaging over MM generated image token sequences, effectively capturing the model’s likelihood of producing each token zz given the prompt xx.

We then calculate a distribution distance, e.g., Jensen-Shannon Divergence(JSD)(Lin, 1991), between the neutral-prompt distribution p^θ​(z∣xneutral)\widehat{p}_{\theta}(z\mid x_{\text{neutral}}) and the augmented-prompt distribution p^θ​(z∣xaug​(di))\widehat{p}_{\theta}(z\mid x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i})). Formally,
where
and M=12​(P+Q)M=\frac{1}{2}(P+Q) is the average distribution, with DKLD_{\mathrm{KL}} denoting the Kullback-Leibler divergence.

We observe that when did_{i} matches the predicted demographic label d^i\hat{d}_{i} predicted from the final images, the JSD between the two distributions is significantly smaller. In other words, the token-level distribution for the neutral prompt xneutralx_{\text{neutral}} is closest to that for xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}) corresponding to the majority demographic group the model actually generated.

This indicates that the language model itself already introduces demographic preferences at the token level. If the prompt “firefighter” leads to a token distribution similar to “male firefighter,” it suggests that the tokens 𝐳i\mathbf{z}_{i} are implicitly biased toward that demographic, even before any visual decoding procedure. Hence, the major source of demographic bias remains in the upstream processes: the autoregressive image token generation. In the following section, we describe the approach we developed to mitigate the debias in image generation by focusing on LM.

We then use a pre-trained image classifier 𝒞\mathcal{C} (Shen et al., 2024) to label each final image yiy_{i} with a predicted attribute d^i=𝒞​(yi)\widehat{d}_{i}\;=\;\mathcal{C}(y_{i}). This yields pairs (xi,yi,d^i,𝐳i)\bigl{(}x_{i},y_{i},\hat{d}_{i},\mathbf{z}_{i}\bigr{)}—i.e. the prompt, the resulting image, the predicted demographic label, and the corresponding image token sequence.

subsections.title: Takeaway
paragraphs: This indicates that the language model itself already introduces demographic preferences at the token level. If the prompt “firefighter” leads to a token distribution similar to “male firefighter,” it suggests that the tokens 𝐳i\mathbf{z}_{i} are implicitly biased toward that demographic, even before any visual decoding procedure. Hence, the major source of demographic bias remains in the upstream processes: the autoregressive image token generation. In the following section, we describe the approach we developed to mitigate the debias in image generation by focusing on LM.

To effectively reduce bias in U-MLLM, a training dataset that explicitly includes diverse demographic attributes is of importance. In general, web-scale data under-represents certain groups or inherently skews toward certain stereotypes. By synthesizing balanced data—where each demographic group appears in similar proportion- we can give the model a stronger signal to reduce its biases during training. This can ensure that the U-MLLM is trained on a controlled distribution counteracts the bias present in real-world data distribution.

subsections.title: 4.1 Training Data Generation
paragraphs: We leveraged diffusion model FLUX.1-dev(Labs, 2023) to synthesize our training data as follows:

1. Base Prompt Selection. We begin by collecting a set of base prompts xn​e​u​t​r​a​lx_{neutral} (for example, “a portrait of a {occupation}”), where occupations are drawn from a publicly available dataset(Shen et al., 2024).

2. Demographic Augmentation. For each base prompt xn​e​u​t​r​a​lx_{neutral}, we augment it with demographic attributes d∈{d1,d2,…}d\in\{d_{1},d_{2},\dots\}, resulting in augmented prompts xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}). For example, starting with the base prompt “a photo of a nurse” we generate augmented prompts such as “a photo of a male nurse” and “a photo of an Asian nurse.”

3. Image Generation via Diffusion Model. For each xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}), we feed it into FLUX.1-dev(Labs, 2023) model to obatin a set of images {y1,y2,…}\{y_{1},y_{2},\dots\}. This step ensures diversity in the visual representations of each demographic group.

4. Paired Dataset Creation. To create the dataset, we pair each generated set of images yky_{k} with its corresponding base prompt xn​e​u​t​r​a​lx_{neutral}. Before pairing, we use the ChatGPT-4o model to paraphrase the base prompt to introduce linguistic diversity while maintaining neutrality. For instance, the base prompt “a photo of a nurse” can be paraphrased as “a photo of an individual in the nursing profession.” The resulting synthetic dataset pairs each neutral prompt with a set of images, where each image corresponds to a specific demographic group:

Demographic Augmentation. For each base prompt xn​e​u​t​r​a​lx_{neutral}, we augment it with demographic attributes d∈{d1,d2,…}d\in\{d_{1},d_{2},\dots\}, resulting in augmented prompts xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}). For example, starting with the base prompt “a photo of a nurse” we generate augmented prompts such as “a photo of a male nurse” and “a photo of an Asian nurse.”

Image Generation via Diffusion Model. For each xaug​(di)x_{\text{aug}}(d_{i}), we feed it into FLUX.1-dev(Labs, 2023) model to obatin a set of images {y1,y2,…}\{y_{1},y_{2},\dots\}. This step ensures diversity in the visual representations of each demographic group.

Paired Dataset Creation. To create the dataset, we pair each generated set of images yky_{k} with its corresponding base prompt xn​e​u​t​r​a​lx_{neutral}. Before pairing, we use the ChatGPT-4o model to paraphrase the base prompt to introduce linguistic diversity while maintaining neutrality. For instance, the base prompt “a photo of a nurse” can be paraphrased as “a photo of an individual in the nursing profession.” The resulting synthetic dataset pairs each neutral prompt with a set of images, where each image corresponds to a specific demographic group:

Because we explicitly injected demographic attributes into the prompts, 𝒟syn\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{syn}} spans multiple genders, ethnicities, and roles—offering more balanced coverage than typical real-world data.

subsections.title: 4.2 Balanced Preference Loss
paragraphs: Existing preference-optimization approaches focus on aligning the model’s response to user-specified preferences (e.g., “prefer polite over rude text”). By contrast, in this study we seek balance among demographic attributes—for instance, the model should equally generate {female, male} or {Asian, Black, Indian, White} under neutral prompts, rather than overwhelmingly generate one demographic group. This shift in perspective means we want the absolute difference in preference across demographics to be minimized, rather than pushing a single demographic to be more or less likely in isolation. To encourage equal representation, as shown in Figure 2, we aim to penalize deviations by minimizing

In other words, if the model is more likely to generate yd1y_{d_{1}} (image with demographic label d1d_{1}) than ydjy_{d_{j}} (image with demographic label djd_{j}), it will be penalized, which encourages balance of the output distribution, and vice versa.
Concretely, let ydi,ydjy_{d_{i}},y_{d_{j}} be images from different demographic groups. We define a balanced odds penalty:

Minimizing (10) can lead to p​(ydi≻ydj∣x)p(y_{d_{i}}\succ y_{d_{j}}\mid x) closer to p​(ydj≻ydi∣x)p(y_{d_{j}}\succ y_{d_{i}}\mid x), thus encouraging the even distribution of generation among demographic attributes.

For race attributes, we have multiple demographic groups. Let’s define KK to be the number of race categories. The auxiliary loss to penalize demographic odds of different attributes deviating from each other:
We adopt a straightforward two-stage procedure as illustrated in Algorithm 1.

1. Supervised Finetuning (SFT). We begin by finetuning the U-MLLM on a supervised dataset of {prompt, image tokens} pairs to ensure high-quality image generation and semantic fidelity. This yields an SFT model πSFT\pi_{\text{SFT}}.
2. Balanced Preference Optimization. We then apply a reference-free odds-ratio penalty via Eq. 11 to make the model equally prefer multiple demographic attributes.

Supervised Finetuning (SFT). We begin by finetuning the U-MLLM on a supervised dataset of {prompt, image tokens} pairs to ensure high-quality image generation and semantic fidelity. This yields an SFT model πSFT\pi_{\text{SFT}}.
Balanced Preference Optimization. We then apply a reference-free odds-ratio penalty via Eq. 11 to make the model equally prefer multiple demographic attributes.

Our approach directly integrates demographic balance into the policy’s objective via Equation 11. By including samples with multiple demographic attributes in the balanced dataset 𝒟bal\mathcal{D}_{\text{bal}}, the model is enabled to generalize towards each demographic group. The balanced preference loss ℒbal\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{bal}} then reduces the inter-difference among demographic groups for the model’s preference. Overall, this two-stage method ensures the model maintains good generation quality (from Stage 1) while significantly reducing absolute preference differences between demographic attributes (from Stage 2).

subsections.title: Two-Stage Training
paragraphs: We adopt a straightforward two-stage procedure as illustrated in Algorithm 1.

1. Supervised Finetuning (SFT). We begin by finetuning the U-MLLM on a supervised dataset of {prompt, image tokens} pairs to ensure high-quality image generation and semantic fidelity. This yields an SFT model πSFT\pi_{\text{SFT}}.
2. Balanced Preference Optimization. We then apply a reference-free odds-ratio penalty via Eq. 11 to make the model equally prefer multiple demographic attributes.

Supervised Finetuning (SFT). We begin by finetuning the U-MLLM on a supervised dataset of {prompt, image tokens} pairs to ensure high-quality image generation and semantic fidelity. This yields an SFT model πSFT\pi_{\text{SFT}}.
Balanced Preference Optimization. We then apply a reference-free odds-ratio penalty via Eq. 11 to make the model equally prefer multiple demographic attributes.

Our approach directly integrates demographic balance into the policy’s objective via Equation 11. By including samples with multiple demographic attributes in the balanced dataset 𝒟bal\mathcal{D}_{\text{bal}}, the model is enabled to generalize towards each demographic group. The balanced preference loss ℒbal\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{bal}} then reduces the inter-difference among demographic groups for the model’s preference. Overall, this two-stage method ensures the model maintains good generation quality (from Stage 1) while significantly reducing absolute preference differences between demographic attributes (from Stage 2).

subsections.title: 5.1 Experimental Setup
paragraphs: In our study, we evaluate the generation bias with respect to gender and race for latest U-MLLMs that can both understand and generate visual content. The models we considered include VILA-U(Wu et al., 2024b), TokenFlow(Qu et al., 2024), Emu3(Wang et al., 2024b), Janus(Wu et al., 2024a), Show-o(Xie et al., 2024), Janus-Pro(Chen et al., 2025). Since VILA-U was the only model with available training code when we began this study, we fine-tuned it into various variants for comparison. For VILA-U, we compare its variants: (1). Prompt engineering: original VILA-U model with a modified prompt to explicitly ask for diversity in image generation. (2). Understanding Finetuning(I →\rightarrow T): VILA-U finetuned on the dataset in image-to-text fashion. (3). Generation Finetuning(T →\rightarrow I): VILA-U finetuned on the same balanced data in text-to-image fashion.(4). Balanced Preference Loss: VILA-U finetuned using our proposed balanced preference optimization for visual generation. (Sec. 4).

For evaluation, we collect a set of occupation-based generation prompts (e.g. “nurse,” “data engineer,” “senator”) that have been used in previous studies(Shen et al., 2024) to assess diffusion models’ biases concerning gender and race. These prompts are 50 in size and are publicly available. We utilized these prompts to evaluate various U-MLLMs under consistent conditions: We generate N=160N=160 images for each test prompt and use an attribute classifier(Shen et al., 2024) to determine the demographic attribute labels.

For training, we collected a separate set of occupation-based generation prompts(Shen et al., 2024) (e.g., “scientist,” “firefighter”) and generated synthetic images using a diffusion model, as described in Section 4. Our dataset comprises 1,000 training prompts, resulting in 10,000 images per demographic group. We consider six demographic groups: male, female, White, Black, Asian, and Indian. Our training dataset consists of 10,000 samples, each containing a text prompt and six images from different demographic groups.
Metrics and Evaluation We follow prior study(Shen et al., 2024), the same bias metrics and image quality metrics are adapted:

• Bias Metrics: Representation Disparity (RD) measures how often each demographic attribute appears in generated outputs given a neutral prompt (e.g., “CEO”). We leverage the classifier model provided by prior study to predict the attributes from generated images. RD is calculated as: where freq​(i)\mathrm{freq}(i) is the frequency of demographic did_{i} in the image generation samples. A lower RD indicates less biased and more balanced demographic representation.

• Image Quality Metrics: we utilized a range of metrics for image quality. (1). CLIP Score Measures alignment between generated image and textual prompt. Higher scores indicate better semantic matching. (2). CLIP IQA: Image quality assessment using CLIP’s visual-textual understanding. Evaluates aspects such as sharpness without reference images. (3). Inception Score: Metric used for evaluating quality and diversity of generated images. Higher scores indicate better quality.

Bias Metrics: Representation Disparity (RD) measures how often each demographic attribute appears in generated outputs given a neutral prompt (e.g., “CEO”). We leverage the classifier model provided by prior study to predict the attributes from generated images. RD is calculated as:
where freq​(i)\mathrm{freq}(i) is the frequency of demographic did_{i} in the image generation samples. A lower RD indicates less biased and more balanced demographic representation.

Image Quality Metrics: we utilized a range of metrics for image quality. (1). CLIP Score Measures alignment between generated image and textual prompt. Higher scores indicate better semantic matching. (2). CLIP IQA: Image quality assessment using CLIP’s visual-textual understanding. Evaluates aspects such as sharpness without reference images. (3). Inception Score: Metric used for evaluating quality and diversity of generated images. Higher scores indicate better quality.

First, we generate N=160N=160 images for each test prompt. Next, we apply a demographic classifier from a previous study (Shen et al., 2024) to predict the labels of demographic attributes for each image. These labeled attributes are then used to compute the overall bias and semantic score based on the above metrics.

Base Learning Rate: We start with a fixed learning rate (for example, 1×10−41\times 10^{-4}) for fine-tuning. Batch Size: Ranges from 8 to 32, depending on the setup. Number of Steps: For the first stage, we fine-tune for up to 10 epochs, checking bias and quality after the training. For the second stage, the epoch is chosen to be 1 to 2. We use the LoRA(Hu et al., 2021) method for finetuning; the rank is 32 for all experiments.

subsections.title: Models.
paragraphs: In our study, we evaluate the generation bias with respect to gender and race for latest U-MLLMs that can both understand and generate visual content. The models we considered include VILA-U(Wu et al., 2024b), TokenFlow(Qu et al., 2024), Emu3(Wang et al., 2024b), Janus(Wu et al., 2024a), Show-o(Xie et al., 2024), Janus-Pro(Chen et al., 2025). Since VILA-U was the only model with available training code when we began this study, we fine-tuned it into various variants for comparison. For VILA-U, we compare its variants: (1). Prompt engineering: original VILA-U model with a modified prompt to explicitly ask for diversity in image generation. (2). Understanding Finetuning(I →\rightarrow T): VILA-U finetuned on the dataset in image-to-text fashion. (3). Generation Finetuning(T →\rightarrow I): VILA-U finetuned on the same balanced data in text-to-image fashion.(4). Balanced Preference Loss: VILA-U finetuned using our proposed balanced preference optimization for visual generation. (Sec. 4).

subsections.title: Evaluation Data.
paragraphs: For evaluation, we collect a set of occupation-based generation prompts (e.g. “nurse,” “data engineer,” “senator”) that have been used in previous studies(Shen et al., 2024) to assess diffusion models’ biases concerning gender and race. These prompts are 50 in size and are publicly available. We utilized these prompts to evaluate various U-MLLMs under consistent conditions: We generate N=160N=160 images for each test prompt and use an attribute classifier(Shen et al., 2024) to determine the demographic attribute labels.

subsections.title: Finetuning Data.
paragraphs: For training, we collected a separate set of occupation-based generation prompts(Shen et al., 2024) (e.g., “scientist,” “firefighter”) and generated synthetic images using a diffusion model, as described in Section 4. Our dataset comprises 1,000 training prompts, resulting in 10,000 images per demographic group. We consider six demographic groups: male, female, White, Black, Asian, and Indian. Our training dataset consists of 10,000 samples, each containing a text prompt and six images from different demographic groups.
Metrics and Evaluation We follow prior study(Shen et al., 2024), the same bias metrics and image quality metrics are adapted:

• Bias Metrics: Representation Disparity (RD) measures how often each demographic attribute appears in generated outputs given a neutral prompt (e.g., “CEO”). We leverage the classifier model provided by prior study to predict the attributes from generated images. RD is calculated as: where freq​(i)\mathrm{freq}(i) is the frequency of demographic did_{i} in the image generation samples. A lower RD indicates less biased and more balanced demographic representation.

• Image Quality Metrics: we utilized a range of metrics for image quality. (1). CLIP Score Measures alignment between generated image and textual prompt. Higher scores indicate better semantic matching. (2). CLIP IQA: Image quality assessment using CLIP’s visual-textual understanding. Evaluates aspects such as sharpness without reference images. (3). Inception Score: Metric used for evaluating quality and diversity of generated images. Higher scores indicate better quality.

where freq​(i)\mathrm{freq}(i) is the frequency of demographic did_{i} in the image generation samples. A lower RD indicates less biased and more balanced demographic representation.

Image Quality Metrics: we utilized a range of metrics for image quality. (1). CLIP Score Measures alignment between generated image and textual prompt. Higher scores indicate better semantic matching. (2). CLIP IQA: Image quality assessment using CLIP’s visual-textual understanding. Evaluates aspects such as sharpness without reference images. (3). Inception Score: Metric used for evaluating quality and diversity of generated images. Higher scores indicate better quality.

subsections.title: Evaluation Protocol.
paragraphs: First, we generate N=160N=160 images for each test prompt. Next, we apply a demographic classifier from a previous study (Shen et al., 2024) to predict the labels of demographic attributes for each image. These labeled attributes are then used to compute the overall bias and semantic score based on the above metrics.

subsections.title: Training Procedure.
paragraphs: Base Learning Rate: We start with a fixed learning rate (for example, 1×10−41\times 10^{-4}) for fine-tuning. Batch Size: Ranges from 8 to 32, depending on the setup. Number of Steps: For the first stage, we fine-tune for up to 10 epochs, checking bias and quality after the training. For the second stage, the epoch is chosen to be 1 to 2. We use the LoRA(Hu et al., 2021) method for finetuning; the rank is 32 for all experiments.

subsections.title: 5.2 Experimental Results
paragraphs: Table 1 summarizes the results for generation bias evaluation on a range of U-MLLMs and especially the VILA-U model with a few debiasing strategies applied. We measure gender bias, race bias, and their intersection (G.×\timesR.), together with semantics-preservation metrics. Lower bias scores indicate more fairness, while higher semantics scores indicate more fidelity in the generation.

As shown in Table 1, Stable Diffusion exhibits moderate gender bias (0.67) and race bias (0.42). Among U-MLLMs, Janus-series (Janus, Janus-Pro) show higher overall bias in gender (0.87–0.90), as well as high race and G×R scores (0.43–0.48 and 0.23–0.24). Show-o and TokenFlow both witness slightly lower bias. Compared with other U-MLLMs, Emu3 stands out with low gender (0.83) and G×R (0.22) biases, along with good semantic scores. Finally, VILA-U exhibits strong text–image alignment (CLIP-S of 28.24) but has high gender (0.89) and intersectional (0.24) bias, highlighting the need for reducing bias in U-MLLMs.

Table 1 presents four methods to reduce gender bias in VILA-U. Prompt Engineering witness moderate bias reduction (Gender = 0.56) while preserving high semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.51). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) achieved the smallest reduction in gender bias (0.83) even though it witnessed a little increase in semantics (CLIP-S = 28.49). By contrast, Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) drastically lowers the gender bias to 0.27. However, it came with the side effect that image quality is lower (CLIP-IQA = 0.77). Finally, BPO (Balanced Preference Optimization) further lowers gender bias (0.25) while preserving reasonable visual fidelity (Inception = 2.10), indicating that our approach is effective for mitigating stereotypical associations without compromising generation quality for U-MLLMs.

Table 1 shows the results for mitigating race bias in the VILA-U. Prompt Engineering achieves a moderate bias reduction (Race = 0.49) while retaining a high level of semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.51). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) yields Race = 0.44 and slightly lower semantic scores (CLIP-S = 28.14). By contrast, Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) significantly reduces the race bias to 0.23, albeit with some drop in semantic fidelity (Inception = 1.98). BPO also lower the race bias a lot(0.26), and achieved good semantic fidelity (Inception 2.31) in the meantime.

As shown under Debias: G×\timesR in Table 1, we evaluate methods targeting both gender and race simultaneously. Prompt Engineering achieves a moderately low intersectional bias (G×\timesR = 0.18) while preserving acceptable semantic fidelity (CLIP-S = 28.09). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) reduces intersectional bias to 0.23, accompanied by strong semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.34), whereas Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) further lowers G×\timesR to 0.17 but at the cost of slightly reduced image quality (Inception = 1.91). BPO achieved the lowest bias score in gender-race intersection as 0.15. It also achieved high semantic fidelity with an Inception score of 2.06.

subsections.title: Baseline Models.
paragraphs: As shown in Table 1, Stable Diffusion exhibits moderate gender bias (0.67) and race bias (0.42). Among U-MLLMs, Janus-series (Janus, Janus-Pro) show higher overall bias in gender (0.87–0.90), as well as high race and G×R scores (0.43–0.48 and 0.23–0.24). Show-o and TokenFlow both witness slightly lower bias. Compared with other U-MLLMs, Emu3 stands out with low gender (0.83) and G×R (0.22) biases, along with good semantic scores. Finally, VILA-U exhibits strong text–image alignment (CLIP-S of 28.24) but has high gender (0.89) and intersectional (0.24) bias, highlighting the need for reducing bias in U-MLLMs.

subsections.title: Debiasing Gender.
paragraphs: Table 1 presents four methods to reduce gender bias in VILA-U. Prompt Engineering witness moderate bias reduction (Gender = 0.56) while preserving high semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.51). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) achieved the smallest reduction in gender bias (0.83) even though it witnessed a little increase in semantics (CLIP-S = 28.49). By contrast, Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) drastically lowers the gender bias to 0.27. However, it came with the side effect that image quality is lower (CLIP-IQA = 0.77). Finally, BPO (Balanced Preference Optimization) further lowers gender bias (0.25) while preserving reasonable visual fidelity (Inception = 2.10), indicating that our approach is effective for mitigating stereotypical associations without compromising generation quality for U-MLLMs.

subsections.title: Debiasing Race.
paragraphs: Table 1 shows the results for mitigating race bias in the VILA-U. Prompt Engineering achieves a moderate bias reduction (Race = 0.49) while retaining a high level of semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.51). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) yields Race = 0.44 and slightly lower semantic scores (CLIP-S = 28.14). By contrast, Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) significantly reduces the race bias to 0.23, albeit with some drop in semantic fidelity (Inception = 1.98). BPO also lower the race bias a lot(0.26), and achieved good semantic fidelity (Inception 2.31) in the meantime.

subsections.title: Debiasing Intersectional Attributes (G×\timesR).
paragraphs: As shown under Debias: G×\timesR in Table 1, we evaluate methods targeting both gender and race simultaneously. Prompt Engineering achieves a moderately low intersectional bias (G×\timesR = 0.18) while preserving acceptable semantic fidelity (CLIP-S = 28.09). Finetune (I →\rightarrow T) reduces intersectional bias to 0.23, accompanied by strong semantic alignment (CLIP-S = 28.34), whereas Finetune (T →\rightarrow I) further lowers G×\timesR to 0.17 but at the cost of slightly reduced image quality (Inception = 1.91). BPO achieved the lowest bias score in gender-race intersection as 0.15. It also achieved high semantic fidelity with an Inception score of 2.06.

subsections.title: 5.3 Discussion
paragraphs: In Figure 3, we compare the VILA-U model with our method in different debiasing dimensions. Our approach evidently enables the model to generate more diverse samples. Our method effectively mitigates stereotypes and reduces the risk of perpetuating inequalities in automatically generated content.

We examine visual understanding bias to investigate bias in multimodal language models further. Specifically, we provide each U-MLLM with a prompt of the form “What is the gender of {occupation}? Answer with options male, female, unknown.” while providing an input empty image to ensure a fair comparison. We then sample 100 responses per prompt under a fixed sampling strategy. From these textual outputs, we extract the demographic attributes that each model most frequently associates with a given occupation. Then we use R​DRD metrics to calculate the bias.

Results in Figure 4 indicate that most models exhibit a pronounced understanding bias; they systematically infer one gender or demographic for an occupation even when provided no actual visual cues. Interestingly, we observe cases of partial alignment. For example, while the Janus model shows a moderate understanding bias in its responses—often refusing to label an individual’s gender or race explicitly, it nonetheless exhibits a substantial generation bias when prompted to create images. In other words, the model’s understanding-level alignment (refusal to answer demographic queries) does not carry over to its image-generation behavior, which remains biased. This highlights that merely aligning a model’s visual understanding to minimize bias in VQA scenarios is insufficient if its generative capability still systematically favors particular demographics.

Prior work(Tong et al., 2024) suggests that enhancing a model’s capability for image understanding can indirectly improve its performance in image generation. Motivated by this, we use the diffusion-generated images and their demographic labels (derived from prompts) to train the model in a discriminative fashion. The resulting model fails to reduce demographic bias in image generation. In other words, the ability to classify or reason about demographic attributes in images does not carry over to balanced generation of images with varied demographics.

These findings imply that bias in understanding (e.g., naming attributes) and bias in generation (e.g., synthesizing certain demographics disproportionately) may arise from distinct internal mechanisms. Whereas understanding tasks rely on discriminative representations, generative tasks involve autoregressive sampling or likelihood maximization that can perpetuate different stereotypes. Therefore, improving one aspect of visual-linguistic alignment does not necessarily resolve the other. Designing more advanced methods that holistically address both comprehension and production biases is essential for fair multimodal generation.

Table 2 shows the results of applying similar finetuning strategies to TokenFlow. While TokenFlow initially exhibits a high gender bias (0.84), simple interventions such as prompting engineering reduce its bias to 0.69. Finetuning approach further lowers it to 0.55, at a minor cost in semantics scores. These findings suggest that finetuning can be extended beyond VILA-U to other U-MLLM toward fairer image generation.

subsections.title: Qualitive Samples
paragraphs: In Figure 3, we compare the VILA-U model with our method in different debiasing dimensions. Our approach evidently enables the model to generate more diverse samples. Our method effectively mitigates stereotypes and reduces the risk of perpetuating inequalities in automatically generated content.

subsections.title: Is there understanding bias in U-MLLMs?
paragraphs: We examine visual understanding bias to investigate bias in multimodal language models further. Specifically, we provide each U-MLLM with a prompt of the form “What is the gender of {occupation}? Answer with options male, female, unknown.” while providing an input empty image to ensure a fair comparison. We then sample 100 responses per prompt under a fixed sampling strategy. From these textual outputs, we extract the demographic attributes that each model most frequently associates with a given occupation. Then we use R​DRD metrics to calculate the bias.

Results in Figure 4 indicate that most models exhibit a pronounced understanding bias; they systematically infer one gender or demographic for an occupation even when provided no actual visual cues. Interestingly, we observe cases of partial alignment. For example, while the Janus model shows a moderate understanding bias in its responses—often refusing to label an individual’s gender or race explicitly, it nonetheless exhibits a substantial generation bias when prompted to create images. In other words, the model’s understanding-level alignment (refusal to answer demographic queries) does not carry over to its image-generation behavior, which remains biased. This highlights that merely aligning a model’s visual understanding to minimize bias in VQA scenarios is insufficient if its generative capability still systematically favors particular demographics.

subsections.title: Can debiasing understanding help debias generation?
paragraphs: Prior work(Tong et al., 2024) suggests that enhancing a model’s capability for image understanding can indirectly improve its performance in image generation. Motivated by this, we use the diffusion-generated images and their demographic labels (derived from prompts) to train the model in a discriminative fashion. The resulting model fails to reduce demographic bias in image generation. In other words, the ability to classify or reason about demographic attributes in images does not carry over to balanced generation of images with varied demographics.

These findings imply that bias in understanding (e.g., naming attributes) and bias in generation (e.g., synthesizing certain demographics disproportionately) may arise from distinct internal mechanisms. Whereas understanding tasks rely on discriminative representations, generative tasks involve autoregressive sampling or likelihood maximization that can perpetuate different stereotypes. Therefore, improving one aspect of visual-linguistic alignment does not necessarily resolve the other. Designing more advanced methods that holistically address both comprehension and production biases is essential for fair multimodal generation.

subsections.title: Generalization to other models.
paragraphs: Table 2 shows the results of applying similar finetuning strategies to TokenFlow. While TokenFlow initially exhibits a high gender bias (0.84), simple interventions such as prompting engineering reduce its bias to 0.69. Finetuning approach further lowers it to 0.55, at a minor cost in semantics scores. These findings suggest that finetuning can be extended beyond VILA-U to other U-MLLM toward fairer image generation.

subsections.title: Multimodal Generative Models
paragraphs: Unified multimodal large language models (U-MLLMs) have advanced the start-of-the-art by bridging visual understanding and conditional generation capabilities. Compared to early MLLMs, which focus purely on understanding, such as Llava series (Liu et al., 2024, 2023), these more recent works, represented by VILA-U (Wu et al., 2024b), Show-o (Xie et al., 2024), MetaMorph (Tong et al., 2024), TokenFlow (Qu et al., 2024), Emu3 (Wang et al., 2024b), TokenFusion (Zhou et al., 2024), Janus (Wu et al., 2024a; Ma et al., 2024), and etc. (Bachmann et al., 2024; Le et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024d), highlight their effectiveness in generating high-quality visuals conditioned on text prompts. U-MLLMs usually employ autoregressive paradigms that may inherit or amplify the biases embedded in their training data. Existing studies predominantly focus on performance improvement rather than understanding and addressing the more critical issues such as demographic fairness.

subsections.title: Fairness in Image Generation
paragraphs: The social risks of biased image generation, particularly gender and racial disparities, have been extensively documented (Kotek et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024a, b). Prior efforts in diffusion-based models attempted to mitigate bias by re-balancing training data and incorporating fairness objectives (Kim et al., 2024). However, these approaches are not directly transferable to UMLLMs due to architectural differences and tokenization mechanisms. Few studies explore bias sources within unified models or their downstream effects on generated outputs, leaving a gap in understanding the interaction between text and image modalities.

subsections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: We examined demographic bias (e.g., gender, race) in unified multimodal large language models (U-MLLMs). We proposed a balanced preference optimization framework that combines supervised finetuning with a preference deviation penalty. Our experiments show that this method effectively reduces bias while maintaining high image quality and semantic fidelity. Moreover, our analysis of understanding versus generation bias underscores the importance of addressing both visual reasoning and token-level preference shaping for comprehensive alignment. We hope these findings will guide future work toward building multimodal models with stronger fairness guarantees. Our work also have some limitation as illustrated in Appendix F.

subsections.title: Impact Statement.
paragraphs: Our work addresses the challenge of demographic bias in unified multimodal language models, aiming to ensure fair image generation for different demographic groups. Our methods can help mitigate harmful stereotypes and minimize the risk of reinforcing inequalities in automatically generated content. We believe ongoing work from researchers, policymakers, and the communities represented is important to maximize the societal benefits of debiasing techniques while maintaining transparency and fairness in machine learning systems.
This section represents the structure of U-MLLM and visual tokenizer; all the content in this section is from prior study(Wu et al., 2024b).

[Figure: Figure 5: Overview of framework’s multi-modal training and inference process(Wu et al., 2024b) Visual inputs are converted into discrete tokens and merged with textual tokens to create a unified multi-modal token sequence. This sequence is used in next-token prediction process, which supports a unified training objective. During inference, output tokens are processed through either text detokenizer or vision tower decoder, generating multi-modal content outputs(Wu et al., 2024b).]

[Figure: Figure 6: Overview of unified foundation vision tower(Wu et al., 2024b) Input images are processed by the vision encoder, where features are extracted and discretized using residual quantization. These discrete vision features are then utilized in two ways: they are fed into the vision decoder to reconstruct images and are used to perform text-image alignment. Throughout this process, both the reconstruction loss and contrastive loss are calculated to refine the vision tower, enabling it to generate discrete visual features that are aligned with text(Wu et al., 2024b).]
[Figure: Figure 7: Detecting bias in LM(top), Vision encoder(bottom); ]

subsections.title: Preference Optimization
paragraphs: Direct preference optimization (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2024) has emerged as a promising technique to address biases in machine learning models, especially LLM. Since then, numerous new loss functions have been proposed (Meng et al., 2024; Park et al., 2024; Hong et al., 2024; Ethayarajh et al., 2024; Azar et al., 2023). Recent advances (Amini et al., 2024) integrate preference modeling into loss functions to guide models toward balanced outputs, which have rarely been explored for MLLMs. Based on this, we introduce a novel balanced preference loss tailored for U-MLLMs. By leveraging demographic attributes during training, the proposed method balances the likelihood of generating outputs across groups without compromising the image quality.
[Table: Table 2: Comparison of Gender Bias and Semantics Preservation Metrics for TokenFlow model.]

subsections.title: Limitation
paragraphs: Several questions remain open despite the bias reduction achieved by our approach. First, our study primarily centered on overt demographic categories (e.g., gender, race). Real-world scenarios may demand addressing intersectional or nuanced attributes (e.g., age, culture, or religion). Second, many models are not fully open-source, restricting the scope of our evaluations to two publicly available systems. Future research could broaden the range of tested models. Lastly, due to resource constraints, we did not explore alternative preference optimization objectives beyond our current framework. Building on our method to incorporate other debiasing approaches is a promising direction for future work.
